title: IICRC Equipment Placement Ratios — Air Movers, Dehumidifiers & Air Scrubbers for Structural Drying
description: Master the IICRC S500 equipment ratios used in Australian structural drying — 1 air mover per 15 m², 1 dehumidifier per 40 m², and air scrubber calculations. RestoreAssist calculates equipment quantities automatically from your damage class and affected area.
author: Phill McGurk | RestoreAssist
transcript:
## Why Equipment Ratios Matter

The quantity of drying equipment placed on a water damage job is not a matter of guesswork or convenience — it is a technical calculation governed by the physics of evaporation and the guidelines in IICRC S500:2025. Under-equipping a job prolongs drying time and increases the risk of secondary mould growth. Over-equipping without justification can attract scrutiny from insurance adjusters and claims of padding.

Correctly calculated and documented equipment ratios are your defence on both fronts.

## Air Mover Ratios

The standard IICRC S500 starting ratio for air movers is one unit per 15–18 m² of affected floor area for a Class 2 loss with carpet and cushion affected. This ratio is sometimes expressed as one air mover per room perimeter wall section (approximately 3–4 linear metres of baseboard).

For Class 3 losses where walls above 60 cm are affected, additional air movers are required for wall-directed drying — typically one additional unit per 9–10 m² of wall cavity area. For Class 4 losses involving dense materials, directed placement at the material surface is required and the ratio may increase further.

Air movers must be positioned in a consistent pattern — offset serpentine along perimeter walls, angled at 45° to direct airflow under carpet or along the wall surface. Random placement significantly reduces drying efficiency even with the correct number of units.

## Dehumidifier Ratios

The IICRC S500:2025 Appendix B guideline for dehumidifiers is one standard refrigerant unit per 40 m² of affected area for a Class 2 loss, or one LGR unit per 50–60 m² due to the higher removal capacity of LGR equipment. These ratios assume ambient temperatures above 16°C.

In Australian winter conditions — particularly in Victoria, Tasmania, and southern New South Wales — ambient temperatures may fall below the operating range of refrigerant dehumidifiers (typically 7–13°C minimum). In these conditions, desiccant dehumidifiers are required, as they operate efficiently at temperatures as low as 1°C.

Dehumidifier placement should be central to the affected area, away from air mover exhaust streams that carry high-humidity air directly past the inlet. For large areas, multiple units distributed across the space outperform a single large unit in one corner.

## Air Scrubber Ratios

Air scrubbers are required on Category 2 and Category 3 jobs, and on any job where mould growth is present or suspected. The IICRC S500 and S520 guideline is a minimum air change rate of 4 air changes per hour (ACH) for the affected space.

Calculation: Room volume (m³) ÷ scrubber capacity (m³/h) × 60 = minutes per air change. Target: ≤ 15 minutes per air change (4 ACH).

For a 30 m² room with a 2.7 m ceiling, volume = 81 m³. A scrubber rated at 600 m³/h achieves approximately 7.4 ACH — well above the minimum. One unit is sufficient. For larger or more contaminated spaces, additional units are placed to maintain 4 ACH throughout.

## Electrical Load Compliance

Under AS/NZS 3012:2019, the total electrical load of drying equipment must not exceed 80% of the circuit capacity. Before deploying equipment, calculate the total ampere draw of all units and confirm it does not exceed 80% of the rated circuit breaker amperage. Distributing equipment across multiple circuits is standard practice on larger jobs.
videoScript:
G'day, welcome to RestoreAssist. Today we're running through equipment placement ratios — the IICRC S500 numbers that tell you how many air movers, dehumidifiers, and air scrubbers to place on a water damage job.

These ratios matter for two reasons: under-equipping slows drying and risks mould, and over-equipping without documentation invites insurance scrutiny. Get the ratios right and documented, and you're protected on both ends.

Starting with air movers. The IICRC S500 baseline for a Class 2 loss — that's carpet and cushion affected, walls up to 60 centimetres — is one air mover per 15 to 18 square metres of affected floor area. Some people use perimeter wall sections instead: one air mover per 3 to 4 linear metres of baseboard.

For Class 3, where water has gone above 60 centimetres into the walls, you add directed air movers for the wall cavities — roughly one per 9 to 10 square metres of affected wall area. The angle and placement pattern matter too — we'll cover that in detail in the air mover placement video, but the short version is: 45 degrees to the surface, offset serpentine pattern around the perimeter.

Dehumidifiers. The IICRC S500 baseline is one standard refrigerant unit per 40 square metres for a Class 2 loss. LGR units have higher removal capacity, so you can stretch that to 50 to 60 square metres per unit. One important note for Australian restorers in cooler climates: refrigerant dehumidifiers stop working effectively below about 13 degrees Celsius. In winter in Victoria or Tasmania, you need desiccant units — they operate down to 1 degree.

Air scrubbers. Required on Category 2, Category 3, and any job with suspected mould. Target a minimum of 4 air changes per hour in the affected space. The calculation is room volume in cubic metres divided by scrubber capacity in cubic metres per hour, multiplied by 60 — that gives you minutes per air change. You want 15 minutes or less.

One more thing to check before you switch everything on: AS/NZS 3012:2019 limits your total electrical load to 80% of circuit capacity. Add up the ampere draw for all your units before you plug in, and spread across circuits if needed.

In RestoreAssist, the equipment calculator does all of this for you. Enter your damage class and affected area, and it calculates the equipment quantities, checks the electrical load against the 80% rule, and generates the scope line items with IICRC justification.

Head to restoreassist.com.au to try it.
